questions.question: In the Volcano iterator model, which three methods does every operator implement?
options: init(), process(), finalize()
start(), get(), stop()
open(), next(), close()
begin(), fetch(), end()
questions.explanation: The Volcano model defines every operator with open() (initialize state and children), next() (return the next tuple or EOF), and close() (clean up). Tuples flow one-at-a-time up the tree via repeated next() calls.

questions.question: What is the primary advantage of the vectorized (batch) execution model over the tuple-at-a-time iterator model?
options: Vectorized models use less memory because batches are compressed
Vectorized models amortize function call overhead, enable SIMD instructions, and have better cache behavior by processing arrays of tuples
Vectorized models are easier to implement for all join algorithms
Vectorized models eliminate the need for query optimization
questions.explanation: Processing batches of tuples (e.g., 1024 at a time) reduces the number of function calls by 1024x, allows tight loops over contiguous arrays that the CPU can SIMD-vectorize, and keeps the cache hotter since operator code runs uninterrupted for thousands of tuples.

questions.question: Which execution model does PostgreSQL primarily use?
options: Push-based dataflow execution
Fully vectorized columnar batch execution
The Volcano iterator model (pull-based, tuple-at-a-time)
Compiled JIT execution only
questions.explanation: PostgreSQL uses the Volcano iterator model. Every plan node (SeqScan, IndexScan, HashJoin, etc.) implements an Exec* function that returns one tuple per call via the standard next() interface.

questions.question: What is a pipeline-breaking operator, and why does it matter for execution models?
options: An operator that crashes the database when given invalid input
An operator like Sort or HashJoin that must consume all input before producing any output, forcing a materialization boundary in the pipeline
An operator that only works with columnar storage formats
An operator that splits data across multiple CPU cores
questions.explanation: Sort and HashJoin are pipeline-breakers: they must see all input (to sort or build a hash table) before emitting any output. In the Volcano model, Sort is simply a blocking operator that materializes all child tuples in open(). In push-based execution, pipeline breakers are explicit exchange points where dataflow changes direction.

questions.question: ClickHouse achieves analytical query speed primarily through:
options: Using only hash indexes for all data access
A fully vectorized columnar execution engine that processes data in column-oriented batches with SIMD instructions
Caching all query results indefinitely
Using the traditional Volcano iterator model with faster hardware
questions.explanation: ClickHouse is built on a fully vectorized columnar execution engine. Instead of processing rows one at a time, it reads column batches (blocks) and applies operations to entire column arrays using SIMD instructions, making it extremely fast for analytical aggregations and scans.

questions.question: In a push-based execution model, how does data flow between operators compared to the pull-based Volcano model?
options: Both models use identical pull-based semantics; only the API names differ
In push-based, data flows from the root down to the leaves
In push-based, child operators push tuples to parent operators via callbacks or exchange buffers, enabling tighter compiled pipelines
Push-based execution is only used for write operations, never for reads
questions.explanation: In push-based execution, the child operator pushes its output tuples into the parent operator (often via a callback or exchange buffer). This allows the database to compile a complete operator pipeline into a single tight loop, eliminating function call overhead entirely. Systems like Hyper and Umbra use this approach combined with JIT compilation.
